basic_information.definition: Leukemia is a type of cancer that originates in the blood-forming tissues, primarily the bone marrow, leading to the production of abnormal white blood cells (leukocytes) that do not function properly.
basic_information.overview: Leukemia is a group of hematological malignancies characterized by the uncontrolled proliferation of immature or abnormal blood cells. It can be acute or chronic and involves either myeloid or lymphoid cell lineages. Leukemic cells crowd out normal blood cells, impairing immune function, oxygen transport, and clotting ability. It is among the most common cancers in children and adults worldwide.

causes: Genetic mutations in hematopoietic stem cells
Chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., Philadelphia chromosome in CML)
Exposure to ionizing radiation
Exposure to carcinogenic chemicals (e.g., benzene)
Prior chemotherapy or radiation therapy
Certain viral infections (e.g., HTLV-1 for adult T-cell leukemia)
risk_factors: Genetic disorders (Down syndrome, Fanconi anemia, Bloom syndrome)
Previous cancer treatment (alkylating agents, topoisomerase II inhibitors)
Age (risk increases with age for most types; ALL peaks in childhood)

pathophysiology.disease_mechanism: Leukemia arises from a single abnormal hematopoietic progenitor cell that acquires oncogenic mutations leading to uncontrolled proliferation and impaired differentiation. These leukemic blasts accumulate in the bone marrow and peripheral blood, crowding out normal hematopoietic cells and causing pancytopenia (anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia).
cellular_changes: Chromosomal translocations causing fusion oncogenes (e.g., BCR-ABL1 in CML)
Loss of tumor suppressor gene function (TP53, RB1)
Epigenetic dysregulation (DNA methylation, histone modification)
Activation of pro-proliferative signaling pathways (JAK-STAT, PI3K-AKT)
Block in myeloid or lymphoid differentiation
Resistance to apoptosis due to BCL-2 overexpression

types.AML: Acute Myeloid Leukemia — rapid progression, immature myeloid blasts; most common acute leukemia in adults.
types.ALL: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia — rapid progression, immature lymphoid blasts; most common cancer in children.
types.CML: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia — slow progression, associated with BCR-ABL1 (Philadelphia chromosome); has chronic, accelerated, and blast phases.
types.CLL: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia — slow progression, accumulation of mature-appearing but dysfunctional B lymphocytes; most common leukemia in adults in Western countries.
types.Other: Hairy Cell Leukemia, T-cell prolymphocytic leukemia, large granular lymphocytic leukemia

common_symptoms: Recurrent infections due to immune failure
advanced_symptoms: CNS involvement (headache, seizures, vision changes) in ALL
Leukostasis (extremely high WBC causing vascular occlusion)

blood_tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC) with differential — elevated WBC, decreased RBC, decreased platelets
Peripheral blood smear — identification of blasts, Auer rods (AML)
Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) — elevated, reflects tumor burden
Uric acid — elevated due to rapid cell turnover
Coagulation panel (PT, aPTT, fibrinogen) — for DIC screening
Comprehensive metabolic panel
Flow cytometry — immunophenotyping of leukemic cells
imaging: Chest X-ray — mediastinal mass (ALL), infections
CT scan of chest/abdomen/pelvis — lymphadenopathy, organomegaly
MRI brain/spine — CNS involvement
PET scan — disease extent in some cases
laboratory_tests.biopsy: Bone marrow aspiration and trephine biopsy — definitive diagnostic procedure; assesses cellularity, blast percentage, morphology
laboratory_tests.histopathological_examination: H&E staining, special stains (myeloperoxidase, PAS), immunohistochemistry (CD markers), cytogenetics (karyotype), FISH, molecular PCR for gene mutations

microscopic_findings: AML: Presence of ≥20% myeloid blasts in bone marrow; Auer rods (pink crystalline inclusions) in myeloid blasts
ALL: Sheets of lymphoblasts with scant cytoplasm, fine chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli
CML: Hypercellular marrow with all myeloid stages; increased basophils and eosinophils
CLL: Effacement of marrow architecture by small, mature-appearing lymphocytes with 'smudge cells' on peripheral smear
tissue_changes: Replacement of normal hematopoietic elements by leukemic infiltrate
Fat cell displacement in hypercellular marrow
cellular_characteristics: High nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio
Prominent or multiple nucleoli
Irregular nuclear contours
Mitotic figures (indicating rapid proliferation)
Aberrant immunophenotype (expression of lineage-inappropriate markers)

diagnostic_biomarkers: CD19, CD20, CD23, CD5 — CLL (B-cell markers)
CD13, CD33, CD117, MPO — AML (myeloid markers)
CD10, CD19, TdT — ALL (B-lymphoblastic markers)
CD3, CD7, TdT — T-ALL markers
BCR-ABL1 transcript — CML and Ph+ ALL
prognostic_biomarkers: FLT3-ITD mutation (poor prognosis in AML)
NPM1 mutation (favorable in AML without FLT3-ITD)
CEBPA biallelic mutation (favorable AML)
TP53 mutation (poor prognosis)
ZAP-70, CD38 expression (poor prognosis in CLL)
Minimal residual disease (MRD) status post-treatment
Cytogenetic risk group (favorable, intermediate, adverse)
genetic_markers: t(9;22) BCR-ABL1 — Philadelphia chromosome (CML, Ph+ ALL)
t(15;17) PML-RARA — AML-M3 (APL), responsive to ATRA
t(8;21) RUNX1-RUNX1T1 — favorable AML
inv(16) CBFB-MYH11 — favorable AML
del(17p), del(11q) — poor prognosis CLL
ETV6-RUNX1 — favorable childhood ALL
MYC rearrangement — aggressive ALL/lymphoma

treatment.first_line_treatment: Induction chemotherapy to achieve complete remission, followed by consolidation and maintenance therapy based on disease type and risk stratification.
medications: Cytarabine (Ara-C) — cornerstone of AML induction
Daunorubicin/Idarubicin — anthracyclines for AML
Vincristine, Prednisone, Asparaginase — ALL induction
Methotrexate — ALL CNS prophylaxis and consolidation
Hydroxyurea — CML cytoreduction
Chlorambucil/Fludarabine — CLL
All-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) — AML-M3 differentiation therapy
treatment.surgery: Not applicable as primary treatment; used for organ complications or diagnostic procedures (splenectomy in select CLL cases).
chemotherapy: AML: '7+3' regimen (7 days Ara-C + 3 days anthracycline)
CML: Previously hydroxyurea + busulfan; now replaced by TKIs
radiotherapy: CNS prophylaxis (cranial irradiation) in high-risk ALL
Total body irradiation (TBI) as conditioning before stem cell transplant
immunotherapy: CAR T-cell therapy (Tisagenlecleucel, Axicabtagene) — relapsed/refractory ALL and CLL
targeted_therapy: Imatinib (Gleevec), Dasatinib, Nilotinib — BCR-ABL1 TKIs for CML
Midostaurin, Gilteritinib — FLT3 inhibitors for AML
Enasidenib, Ivosidenib — IDH2/IDH1 inhibitors for AML
Ibrutinib, Acalabrutinib — BTK inhibitors for CLL
Gemtuzumab ozogamicin — anti-CD33 ADC for AML

short_term: Tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) — electrolyte imbalance, renal failure
long_term: Graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) after stem cell transplant

prevention_methods: Avoid known carcinogens (benzene, pesticides)
Minimize unnecessary radiation exposure
Occupational safety measures in high-risk environments
Genetic counseling for familial predisposition syndromes
lifestyle_modifications: Smoking cessation (reduces AML risk)
Healthy diet and regular exercise (general cancer risk reduction)
Limit alcohol consumption
screening_recommendations: No routine population-based screening available for leukemia
Genetic screening for high-risk individuals (e.g., Down syndrome monitoring)
Surveillance for treatment-related leukemia in prior cancer patients
Regular CBC monitoring in high-risk occupational groups

prognosis.survival_rate: Highly variable by type: ALL in children has >90% 5-year survival with modern treatment; adult AML has ~30-40% 5-year OS; CML on TKI has near-normal life expectancy; CLL is often indolent with median survival >10 years.
prognosis.disease_progression: Acute leukemias progress rapidly within weeks without treatment. CML progresses through chronic, accelerated, and blast crisis phases over months to years. CLL progresses slowly, often monitored without treatment initially ('watch and wait').
prognosis.recurrence: Relapse is the primary cause of treatment failure. AML relapse carries poor prognosis. ALL relapse requires intensive salvage therapy. MRD monitoring guides relapse prediction.

clinical_guidelines.WHO: WHO Classification of Tumours of Haematopoietic and Lymphoid Tissues (5th Edition, 2022) — defines diagnostic criteria and classification for all leukemia subtypes.
clinical_guidelines.NCI: NCI PDQ Cancer Information Summaries — Adult Acute Myeloid Leukemia Treatment; Adult Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia Treatment; Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia Treatment; Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia Treatment. Available at: https://www.cancer.gov/types/leukemia

basic_information.definition: Melanoma is a malignant tumor arising from melanocytes — the pigment-producing cells of the skin (and occasionally mucous membranes, eyes, and other sites). It is the most dangerous form of skin cancer due to its high metastatic potential.
basic_information.overview: Melanoma accounts for only ~1% of skin cancers but causes the majority of skin cancer deaths. It can occur anywhere on the body, including areas not exposed to sunlight. While UV radiation is the primary environmental cause, genetic predispositions play a significant role. Early detection is critical, as localized melanoma has an excellent prognosis whereas metastatic melanoma has historically been fatal, though immunotherapy and targeted therapy have dramatically improved outcomes.

causes: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation exposure (sunlight, tanning beds)
DNA damage in melanocytes leading to BRAF, NRAS, NF1, or KIT mutations
Somatic mutations accumulating in melanocytic nevi
Rare germline mutations (CDKN2A, CDK4, BAP1)
risk_factors: Fair skin, light hair, blue/green eyes
History of sunburns (especially blistering sunburns in childhood)
High UV exposure (geographic latitude, outdoor occupation)
Large number of moles (>50 common nevi)
Personal or family history of melanoma

pathophysiology.disease_mechanism: Melanoma develops from transformed melanocytes that acquire sequential genetic mutations disrupting cell cycle regulation, apoptosis, and invasion. The MAPK signaling pathway (RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK) is activated in most melanomas, predominantly through BRAF V600E mutation. Tumor progression follows a radial growth phase (horizontal spread within epidermis) then vertical growth phase (dermal invasion), enabling metastasis via lymphatics and blood.
cellular_changes: BRAF V600E mutation (~50% of cutaneous melanomas) — constitutive MAPK activation
NRAS mutations (~20%) — activates MAPK and PI3K pathways
NF1 loss-of-function mutations (~15%) — RAS hyperactivation
CDKN2A deletion — loss of p16 tumor suppressor, cell cycle dysregulation
TERT promoter mutation — telomerase reactivation enabling immortalization
Epithelial-mesenchymal transition enabling invasion
MHC downregulation for immune evasion
PD-L1 upregulation — suppression of cytotoxic T-cell response

types.Superficial_Spreading_Melanoma: Most common type (~70%); horizontal radial growth phase; irregular borders and color variation; occurs on trunk (men) and legs (women).
types.Nodular_Melanoma: ~15-20%; aggressive vertical growth from outset; often amelanotic; rapidly enlarging raised nodule.
types.Lentigo_Maligna_Melanoma: ~5-10%; occurs on chronically sun-damaged skin (face, neck) in older adults; slow-growing precursor lentigo maligna.
types.Acral_Lentiginous_Melanoma: Occurs on palms, soles, subungual areas; most common melanoma in dark-skinned populations; associated with KIT mutations.
types.Mucosal_Melanoma: Rare; occurs in oral, nasal, anogenital, or gastrointestinal mucosa; poor prognosis.
types.Uveal_Melanoma: Arises in choroid, ciliary body, or iris; high risk of liver metastasis; associated with GNA11/GNAQ mutations.
types.Desmoplastic_Melanoma: Rare; neurotropic, spindle cell variant; occurs in head/neck; high local recurrence.

early_symptoms: Asymmetric pigmented lesion (ABCDE criteria: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6mm, Evolution/change)
Itching or tingling in existing mole
Darkening or color change in a skin lesion
common_symptoms: Irregular, multicolored pigmented lesion (shades of brown, black, red, white, blue)
Bleeding, ulceration, or crusting of a lesion
advanced_symptoms: Headache, seizures, focal neurological deficits (brain metastases)
Elevated LDH (associated with widespread metastatic disease)

physical_examination: Full skin examination (total body skin exam)
Dermoscopy (dermatoscopy) — enhances visualization of subsurface structures

blood_tests: LDH — prognostic marker in metastatic melanoma
S100B protein — melanoma-specific biomarker
Melanoma inhibitory activity (MIA) protein
CBC and metabolic panel — baseline before systemic therapy
imaging: Ultrasound of regional lymph nodes
CT scan (chest/abdomen/pelvis) — metastasis staging
PET-CT scan — whole-body staging in stage III-IV
MRI brain — detect cerebral metastases
Bone scan — if bone metastasis suspected
laboratory_tests.biopsy: Excisional biopsy with 1-3mm margins — gold standard for primary diagnosis. Incisional biopsy acceptable for large lesions. Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) for tumors >1mm Breslow thickness.
laboratory_tests.histopathological_examination: H&E staining of excised specimen; Breslow thickness measurement (mm); Clark level; ulceration assessment; mitotic rate; lymphovascular invasion; microsatellites; margins assessment

microscopic_findings: Atypical melanocytes at dermal-epidermal junction arranged in irregular nests
Upward (pagetoid) spread of melanocytes into epidermis (superficial spreading type)
Vertical growth phase: atypical melanocytes invading dermis and forming expansile nodule
Nuclear pleomorphism, prominent eosinophilic nucleoli ('owl eyes')
High mitotic rate including atypical mitoses
Ulceration of overlying epidermis
Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) — variable and prognostically relevant
Desmoplastic melanoma: spindle cells in fibrous stroma, neurotropism
tissue_changes: Dermal invasion with displacement of collagen
cellular_characteristics: Epithelioid, spindled, or mixed cell morphology
Abundant finely granular cytoplasm (may be pigmented)
Large, irregular, hyperchromatic nuclei
Prominent nucleoli
Positive for S100, SOX10, HMB-45, Melan-A/MART-1 by IHC

diagnostic_biomarkers: S100B — most sensitive melanoma marker by IHC
HMB-45 (gp100) — melanoma-specific, less sensitive
Melan-A/MART-1 — specific for melanocytic differentiation
SOX10 — highly sensitive transcription factor marker
MiTF (Microphthalmia transcription factor)
PNL2 — additional melanocytic marker
prognostic_biomarkers: Breslow thickness (primary prognostic factor)
Ulceration (worsens prognosis, upstages T category)
Mitotic rate (>/=1/mm² adversely affects prognosis)
LDH level (elevated = stage M1c in metastatic disease)
Sentinel lymph node status
PD-L1 expression (predictive of immunotherapy response)
genetic_markers: BRAF V600E/V600K mutation — present in ~50%; predictive of BRAF inhibitor response
NRAS Q61 mutation — ~20%; may predict MEK inhibitor response
NF1 mutation — ~15%; defines 'triple-wild-type' subgroup
KIT mutation/amplification — acral/mucosal subtypes; imatinib-sensitive
CDKN2A deletion — germline mutation in familial melanoma
GNAQ/GNA11 — uveal melanoma; MEK inhibitor target
TMB (Tumor Mutational Burden) — predictive of immunotherapy response

treatment.first_line_treatment: Wide local excision (WLE) with appropriate surgical margins based on Breslow thickness for localized disease. Systemic therapy (immunotherapy or targeted therapy) for stage III-IV disease.
medications: Pembrolizumab — anti-PD-1 immunotherapy (adjuvant and advanced disease)
Nivolumab — anti-PD-1 immunotherapy
Ipilimumab — anti-CTLA-4 immunotherapy
Dabrafenib + Trametinib — BRAF+MEK inhibitor combination (BRAF-mutant)
Vemurafenib + Cobimetinib — BRAF+MEK inhibitor combination
Encorafenib + Binimetinib — BRAF+MEK inhibitor combination
surgery: Wide local excision (WLE): margins 0.5cm for in situ; 1cm for T1; 1-2cm for T2; 2cm for T3-T4
Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) — for tumors >1mm Breslow thickness
Complete lymph node dissection (CLND) — when SLNB positive (selective use)
chemotherapy: Dacarbazine (DTIC) — historical standard; now rarely used
Temozolomide — oral alkylating agent (cross BBB for CNS disease)
Chemotherapy largely replaced by immunotherapy and targeted therapy
radiotherapy: Adjuvant radiotherapy to nodal basin after high-risk lymph node dissection
Palliative radiation for bone, soft tissue metastases
immunotherapy: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) — FDA-approved for adjuvant stage IIB-IV and advanced melanoma
Nivolumab (anti-PD-1) — FDA-approved for adjuvant and advanced melanoma
Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) — used in combination with nivolumab
Nivolumab + Ipilimumab — superior PFS/OS in metastatic melanoma vs. monotherapy
Talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC) — oncolytic virus (intralesional injection for unresectable stage IIIB-IVM1a)
Relatlimab + Nivolumab (anti-LAG-3 + anti-PD-1) — approved for unresectable/metastatic melanoma
targeted_therapy: BRAF+MEK combination preferred over monotherapy (prevents resistance)
MEK inhibitors (Trametinib, Binimetinib, Selumetinib) — uveal melanoma (investigational)

short_term: Surgical wound complications (infection, hematoma, seroma, dehiscence)
Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) from immunotherapy: colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathy, dermatitis
Skin toxicity from BRAF/MEK inhibitors (rash, photosensitivity, squamous cell carcinomas)
long_term: Secondary cutaneous malignancies (squamous cell carcinoma from vemurafenib)
Permanent endocrine dysfunction (hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency from immunotherapy)
Acquired resistance to targeted therapy (median ~10-12 months)

prevention_methods: UV protection: sunscreen (SPF 30+), protective clothing, hats
Avoid midday sun exposure (10 AM – 4 PM)
Avoidance of tanning beds (linked to ~75% increased risk)
Regular skin self-examination using ABCDE criteria
Genetic counseling for CDKN2A mutation carriers
lifestyle_modifications: Daily broad-spectrum sunscreen application
Wearing UV-protective clothing and wide-brimmed hats
Seeking shade during peak UV hours
Avoiding intentional tanning
Vitamin D supplementation to compensate for sun avoidance
screening_recommendations: Annual total body skin examination by dermatologist for high-risk individuals
Genetic testing and surveillance for CDKN2A mutation families
Dermoscopy-enhanced monitoring for atypical nevi
Monthly self-skin examination recommended by AAD
Ophthalmologic monitoring for uveal melanoma risk factors

prognosis.survival_rate: Stage I: ~95-99% 5-year OS; Stage II: ~65-85%; Stage III: ~40-70% (varies by substage); Stage IV (metastatic): historically <10% at 5 years, now ~50% with modern immunotherapy combinations.
prognosis.disease_progression: Melanoma can progress rapidly from localized to metastatic disease. Common metastatic sites include regional lymph nodes, skin (in-transit/satellite), lung, brain, liver, and bone. Brain metastases occur in up to 50% of patients with advanced disease.
prognosis.recurrence: Recurrence risk correlates with Breslow thickness and lymph node status. Most recurrences occur within 5 years. Adjuvant immunotherapy reduces recurrence risk by ~35-40%. Lifelong surveillance is recommended.

clinical_guidelines.WHO: WHO Cancer Fact Sheet — Skin Cancer/Melanoma. WHO IARC data on melanoma incidence and prevention. Available at: https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/skin-cancers
clinical_guidelines.NCI: NCI PDQ Melanoma Treatment Summary; NCI Melanoma Staging (AJCC 8th Edition). Available at: https://www.cancer.gov/types/skin/patient/melanoma-treatment-pdq

basic_information.definition: Lung cancer is a malignant neoplasm originating from the epithelial cells of the lung. It is the leading cause of cancer-related death worldwide, broadly classified into non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and small cell lung cancer (SCLC).
basic_information.overview: Lung cancer is responsible for more deaths annually than breast, colorectal, and prostate cancers combined. The overwhelming majority (~85%) of cases are attributed to tobacco smoking. Advances in molecular profiling have transformed treatment, with targeted therapies for specific driver mutations (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, MET, RET, KRAS) and immunotherapy significantly improving survival in selected patients.

causes: Tobacco smoking (primary cause; responsible for ~80-85% of lung cancers)
Secondhand smoke exposure
Radon gas exposure (leading cause in non-smokers)
Occupational carcinogens (asbestos, arsenic, chromium, nickel, diesel fumes)
Air pollution (particulate matter PM2.5)
Genetic susceptibility and driver mutations
Prior lung diseases (COPD, pulmonary fibrosis, tuberculosis)
Prior radiation therapy to chest
risk_factors: Tobacco smoking (current or former; pack-year history)
Occupational exposure to asbestos, radon, heavy metals
Living in high air pollution areas
Genetic mutations (EGFR, KRAS germline variants in some families)

pathophysiology.disease_mechanism: Lung carcinogenesis is a multi-step process initiated by carcinogen-induced DNA damage (primarily from tobacco smoke containing 60+ known carcinogens). Key oncogenic pathways include EGFR-RAS-MAPK, PI3K-AKT-mTOR, and ALK signaling. Tumor suppressor inactivation (TP53, RB1, CDKN2A) combined with oncogene activation leads to uncontrolled cell proliferation, invasion, and metastasis.
cellular_changes: KRAS mutations — most common in smokers with adenocarcinoma (~30%)
EGFR activating mutations — exon 19 deletion, exon 21 L858R (~15% overall; higher in Asian non-smokers, women)
ALK rearrangements — EML4-ALK fusion (~5%)
ROS1 rearrangements (~2%)
BRAF V600E mutations (~2-3%)
MET exon 14 skipping mutations (~3-4%)
RET fusions (~2%)
NTRK fusions (rare)
TP53 mutation (most common tumor suppressor loss)
RB1 loss — near universal in SCLC
PD-L1 overexpression — immune checkpoint evasion

NSCLC.Adenocarcinoma: Most common NSCLC (~40% of all lung cancers); peripheral location; associated with EGFR, ALK, ROS1, KRAS mutations; predominant type in non-smokers and women.
NSCLC.Squamous_Cell_Carcinoma: ~25-30% of NSCLC; central location near bronchi; strongly linked to smoking; FGFR1 amplification, DDR2 mutations.
NSCLC.Large_Cell_Carcinoma: ~10-15%; poorly differentiated; diagnosis of exclusion; rapid growth.

types.SCLC: Small Cell Lung Cancer (~15%); neuroendocrine origin; very aggressive; rapid growth; early metastasis; paraneoplastic syndromes; almost exclusively in smokers; classified as limited-stage or extensive-stage.

early_symptoms: Persistent new cough or change in chronic cough
advanced_symptoms: Superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome (facial/arm swelling, plethora)
Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) — Pancoast tumor
Headache, seizures (brain metastases — common in adenocarcinoma, SCLC)
Paraneoplastic syndromes: SIADH (SCLC), Cushing's syndrome, Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome, hypercalcemia (squamous cell)

physical_examination: Decreased breath sounds, dullness to percussion (pleural effusion)

blood_tests: CBC — anemia of chronic disease
Comprehensive metabolic panel — liver function, calcium level
LDH — tumor burden marker
CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen) — elevated in adenocarcinoma
NSE (neuron-specific enolase) — SCLC marker
ProGRP (pro-gastrin-releasing peptide) — SCLC marker
imaging: Chest X-ray — initial evaluation (mass, effusion, atelectasis)
CT chest with contrast — primary tumor characterization, lymph nodes
PET-CT — whole-body staging (M staging)
MRI brain — brain metastasis detection (mandatory before curative intent treatment)
Bone scan — skeletal metastases (if PET not available)
CT-guided biopsy — peripheral lesions
Endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) — mediastinal staging
laboratory_tests.biopsy: Bronchoscopy with BAL, brushings, biopsy — central tumors. CT-guided percutaneous needle biopsy — peripheral lesions. Endobronchial ultrasound-guided biopsy (EBUS-TBNA) — mediastinal/hilar lymph nodes. Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) — pleural disease or diagnostic lobectomy.
laboratory_tests.histopathological_examination: H&E staining, IHC panel (TTF-1, p40/p63, CK5/6, synaptophysin, CD56, chromogranin); molecular profiling for driver mutations; PD-L1 expression by TPS scoring; TMB assessment

microscopic_findings: Adenocarcinoma: acinar, papillary, micropapillary, lepidic, or solid growth patterns; mucin production; TTF-1 positive; lepidic pattern (former BAC) grows along alveolar walls
Squamous cell carcinoma: keratinization, intercellular bridges, pearls; p40/p63/CK5/6 positive
SCLC: sheets of small cells with scant cytoplasm, 'salt and pepper' chromatin, nuclear molding, extensive necrosis; Ki-67 >70%; synaptophysin/CD56/chromogranin positive
Large cell carcinoma: sheets of large pleomorphic cells without squamous/glandular/neuroendocrine differentiation
cellular_characteristics: Adenocarcinoma: columnar cells, glandular architecture, intracytoplasmic mucin
Squamous: polygonal cells, abundant pink cytoplasm, keratin pearls
SCLC: oval cells ~2x lymphocyte size, minimal cytoplasm, crush artifact on biopsy
High mitotic rate in all subtypes

diagnostic_biomarkers: TTF-1 (Thyroid Transcription Factor-1) — adenocarcinoma marker
p40/p63, CK5/6 — squamous cell carcinoma markers
Synaptophysin, Chromogranin A, CD56 — neuroendocrine/SCLC markers
CEA — adenocarcinoma (serum and IHC)
Napsin A — lung adenocarcinoma-specific
prognostic_biomarkers: KRAS mutation — historically poor prognosis; KRASG12C now targetable
STK11/LKB1 mutation — reduced immunotherapy benefit
KEAP1 mutation — reduced immunotherapy benefit, poor prognosis
TP53 mutation — adverse prognosis
Stage (TNM) — most important prognostic factor
Performance status (PS)
PD-L1 TPS ≥50% — predictive of pembrolizumab monotherapy benefit
genetic_markers: EGFR exon 19 del / L858R — sensitive to EGFR TKIs (osimertinib, gefitinib, erlotinib, afatinib)
ALK rearrangement — sensitive to ALK inhibitors (alectinib, crizotinib, brigatinib, lorlatinib)
ROS1 rearrangement — sensitive to crizotinib, entrectinib
BRAF V600E — sensitive to dabrafenib+trametinib
MET exon 14 skipping — sensitive to capmatinib, tepotinib
RET fusion — sensitive to selpercatinib, pralsetinib
NTRK fusion — sensitive to larotrectinib, entrectinib
KRASG12C mutation — sensitive to sotorasib, adagrasib
HER2 mutation — sensitive to trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd)

treatment.first_line_treatment: Stage I-II NSCLC: surgical resection (lobectomy) with curative intent. Stage III NSCLC: concurrent chemoradiation ± durvalumab. Stage IV NSCLC: systemic therapy based on molecular profile and PD-L1 status. SCLC: chemotherapy (etoposide + cisplatin/carboplatin) ± immunotherapy.
medications: Osimertinib — 3rd generation EGFR TKI (standard of care for EGFR+ NSCLC)
Alectinib — ALK inhibitor (standard of care for ALK+ NSCLC)
Pembrolizumab — anti-PD-1 immunotherapy
Atezolizumab — anti-PD-L1 immunotherapy
Durvalumab — anti-PD-L1 (adjuvant unresectable stage III after chemoRT)
Sotorasib/Adagrasib — KRASG12C inhibitors
Cisplatin/Carboplatin + Pemetrexed — standard doublet chemotherapy for adenocarcinoma
Etoposide + Carboplatin — SCLC first-line
surgery: Lobectomy — standard curative surgery for stage I-II NSCLC
Sublobar resection (segmentectomy, wedge resection) — stage I in compromised patients
Video-Assisted Thoracoscopic Surgery (VATS) — minimally invasive approach
Mediastinal lymph node dissection — mandatory for accurate staging
chemotherapy: Pemetrexed — maintenance therapy in non-squamous NSCLC
radiotherapy: Stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR/SBRT) — medically inoperable stage I-II
Concurrent chemoradiotherapy — stage III unresectable NSCLC
Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) — limited-stage SCLC with complete response
Palliative radiotherapy — bone pain, SVC syndrome, brain metastases
immunotherapy: Pembrolizumab monotherapy — PD-L1 TPS ≥50% first-line NSCLC
Pembrolizumab + chemotherapy — PD-L1 any/unselected first-line NSCLC
Atezolizumab + bevacizumab + chemotherapy — first-line non-squamous NSCLC
Durvalumab — adjuvant after concurrent chemoRT in unresectable stage III
Atezolizumab + carboplatin + etoposide — extensive-stage SCLC first-line
targeted_therapy: EGFR inhibitors: Osimertinib (3rd gen), Erlotinib, Gefitinib, Afatinib
ALK inhibitors: Alectinib, Brigatinib, Lorlatinib, Crizotinib

short_term: Post-surgical: air leak, pneumonia, atelectasis, bronchopleural fistula
Chemotherapy toxicity: myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting, nephrotoxicity (cisplatin), neuropathy
long_term: Acquired resistance to targeted therapy (e.g., EGFR T790M, C797S; ALK resistance mutations)
Brain metastases (very common in adenocarcinoma, SCLC)

prevention_methods: Tobacco cessation — single most effective prevention measure
Avoidance of secondhand smoke
Radon testing and mitigation in homes
Occupational safety measures (asbestos abatement, proper respiratory protection)
Air quality improvement policies
lifestyle_modifications: Smoking cessation (reduces risk; never too late)
Diet rich in fruits and vegetables (potential protective effect)
Regular physical activity
Avoiding indoor air pollutants
screening_recommendations: Annual low-dose CT (LDCT) screening for high-risk individuals:
— Age 50-80 years, with ≥20 pack-year smoking history, currently smoking or quit within 15 years (USPSTF 2021 recommendations)
— NLST and NELSON trials demonstrated 20-26% reduction in lung cancer mortality
Smoking cessation counseling should accompany screening programs

prognosis.survival_rate: Stage I: ~60-90% 5-year OS (by subtype); Stage II: ~30-55%; Stage III: ~10-35%; Stage IV: historically <5%, improved to ~20-30% with modern targeted therapy and immunotherapy. SCLC limited-stage: median OS ~15-20 months; extensive-stage: median OS ~10-12 months.
prognosis.disease_progression: Lung cancer typically progresses from locoregional disease to distant metastases involving brain, bone, adrenal glands, and liver. EGFR/ALK-mutant patients have prolonged OS on targeted therapy despite metastatic disease. SCLC is characterized by initial chemosensitivity followed by rapid relapse.
prognosis.recurrence: Post-surgical recurrence in early-stage NSCLC: ~30-50% within 5 years. Adjuvant osimertinib reduces recurrence in EGFR+ stage IB-IIIA resected NSCLC by ~83%. Post-treatment surveillance with CT scans every 6 months for 2 years then annually.

clinical_guidelines.NCI: NCI PDQ Lung Cancer Treatment Summaries (Non-Small Cell and Small Cell); NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology — Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (Version 2024). Available at: https://www.cancer.gov/types/lung

basic_information.definition: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by T-cell mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells, resulting in absolute insulin deficiency and lifelong dependence on exogenous insulin therapy.
basic_information.overview: T1DM accounts for approximately 5-10% of all diabetes cases and typically presents in childhood or young adulthood, though it can occur at any age. The hallmark is the autoimmune obliteration of islet beta cells, leading to severe insulin deficiency. Without insulin, glucose cannot enter cells for energy, and unregulated lipolysis and ketogenesis lead to the acute emergency of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Management requires insulin replacement, continuous glucose monitoring, and lifestyle adjustment.

causes: Autoimmune T-cell mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells (CD4+ and CD8+ T lymphocytes attack beta cell antigens)
Molecular mimicry triggered by environmental factors (viral infections, dietary antigens)
Genetic susceptibility genes enabling aberrant immune response to self-antigens
risk_factors: HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR4 alleles (strongest genetic risk factors; present in >90% of T1DM)
Family history of T1DM (sibling risk ~5-10%; offspring of affected father ~6%)
Certain viral infections (Coxsackievirus B, enteroviruses, rubella) — trigger hypothesis
Geography — higher incidence in Northern Europe (Finland highest globally)
Autoimmune diseases in patient or family (Hashimoto's thyroiditis, Addison's disease, celiac disease)
Early infant dietary factors (cow's milk protein, early introduction of cereals — controversial)

pathophysiology.disease_mechanism: T1DM is characterized by a 'pre-diabetic' phase (months to years) of ongoing beta cell autoimmunity before clinical onset. Autoreactive CD8+ T cells recognize beta cell antigens (insulin, GAD65, IA-2) and destroy beta cells. As beta cell mass declines below a critical threshold (~80-90% loss), insulin secretion becomes inadequate to maintain normoglycemia, leading to hyperglycemia. Absolute insulin deficiency causes unrestrained lipolysis, ketone body formation, and if untreated, DKA.
cellular_changes: Insulitis — mononuclear cell (T cells, macrophages, B cells) infiltration of islets
Progressive beta cell loss; preserved alpha cell (glucagon-secreting) mass
Upregulation of MHC class I on beta cells — increased susceptibility to cytotoxic T-cell killing
Beta cell apoptosis via Fas-FasL pathway and perforin/granzyme release
Pro-inflammatory cytokine release (IL-1β, TNF-α, IFN-γ) causing direct beta cell toxicity
Loss of first-phase insulin secretion — earliest detectable physiological defect

types.Type_1A: Classic autoimmune T1DM with detectable autoantibodies (most common); associated with HLA risk alleles.
types.Type_1B: Idiopathic T1DM — absolute insulin deficiency without evidence of autoimmunity; rare; more common in African and Asian populations.
types.LADA: Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults — adult-onset T1DM misclassified as T2DM; positive autoantibodies; slower beta cell destruction.
types.Stages: Stage 1: autoantibody positive, normoglycemia; Stage 2: autoantibody positive, dysglycemia; Stage 3: clinical T1DM (symptomatic hyperglycemia)

early_symptoms: Unexplained weight loss despite normal or increased appetite
advanced_symptoms: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA): nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul breathing, altered consciousness, coma
Electrolyte imbalances (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia initially then hypokalemia)

physical_examination: Kussmaul respiration (deep rapid breathing) in DKA
Assessment of BMI (typically normal or low in T1DM)

blood_tests: Fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL (7.0 mmol/L) on two occasions
Random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L) with symptoms
HbA1c ≥6.5% (48 mmol/mol) — 3-month glycemic average
C-peptide — very low or undetectable (reflects endogenous insulin secretion)
Autoantibody panel: GADA (anti-GAD65), IA-2A (anti-islet tyrosine phosphatase), IAA (anti-insulin), ZnT8A (anti-zinc transporter 8)
Blood ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) and urine ketones
Arterial blood gas — metabolic acidosis in DKA
Electrolytes, BUN, creatinine — baseline and DKA management
Thyroid function tests (autoimmune comorbidities screening)
imaging: Pancreatic imaging not routinely required for diagnosis
Ultrasound/CT to exclude pancreatic pathology if atypical presentation
laboratory_tests.biopsy: Pancreatic biopsy not performed in clinical practice; insulitis confirmed only in autopsy or research studies.
laboratory_tests.histopathological_examination: Research only: insulitis with mononuclear infiltrate; dramatic reduction in beta cell mass; preserved alpha and delta cells; islet fibrosis in long-standing disease

microscopic_findings: Insulitis: dense infiltration of islets by T lymphocytes, macrophages, and B cells
Selective beta cell destruction with preservation of other islet cells (alpha, delta, PP cells)
Islet atrophy and fibrosis in long-standing T1DM
Ghost islets (fibrotic remnants devoid of beta cells)
Peri-insulitis early stage: immune cells surrounding but not yet invading islets
tissue_changes: Progressive reduction in islet volume and beta cell mass
Replacement of islet tissue by fibrous scar tissue
Increased amyloid deposition (less prominent than T2DM)
Exocrine pancreatic atrophy (pancreas slightly reduced in size)
cellular_characteristics: Beta cells: pyknotic nuclei, apoptotic bodies in active insulitis
Infiltrating cells: CD8+ cytotoxic T cells, CD4+ helper T cells, macrophages, NK cells
MHC class I overexpression on remaining beta cells
Absence of normal dense, granular beta cells by Grimelius stain or insulin IHC

diagnostic_biomarkers: GADA (Glutamic Acid Decarboxylase Antibodies) — most sensitive autoantibody (~80%)
IA-2A (Islet Antigen 2 Antibodies) — ~60% sensitivity
IAA (Insulin Autoantibodies) — most common in young children at onset
ZnT8A (Zinc Transporter 8 Antibodies) — ~60-80% sensitivity
ICA (Islet Cell Antibodies) — historically used, now supplemented by specific antibodies
C-peptide — low/undetectable confirming beta cell failure
prognostic_biomarkers: HbA1c — glycemic control and complication risk
Residual C-peptide — even small amounts associated with better outcomes and reduced DKA risk
eGFR and urine albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) — renal complication monitoring
Lipid panel — cardiovascular risk
Time in range (TIR) >70% by continuous glucose monitor (CGM) — modern glycemic target
genetic_markers: HLA-DR3-DQ2 and HLA-DR4-DQ8 — highest genetic risk haplotypes
HLA-DR15-DQ6 (DQB1*0602) — protective haplotype
INS VNTR — insulin gene region; modifies risk
PTPN22 — protein tyrosine phosphatase; autoimmunity risk gene
CTLA4 — immune regulatory gene
IL2RA (CD25) — T-cell regulation
Polygenic risk scores (PRS) for T1DM prediction

treatment.first_line_treatment: Insulin replacement therapy is mandatory and lifelong. Multiple daily injections (MDI) or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII/insulin pump) are the two main delivery modes.
medications: Rapid-acting insulin analogs: Lispro (Humalog), Aspart (NovoLog), Glulisine (Apidra), Ultra-rapid Fiasp
Long-acting basal insulins: Glargine (Lantus, Toujeo), Detemir (Levemir), Degludec (Tresiba)
Premixed insulins: limited use in T1DM
Teplizumab (Tzield) — FDA-approved (2022) anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody to delay clinical T1DM in Stage 2 high-risk individuals

insulin_therapy.multiple_daily_injections: Basal-bolus regimen: long-acting insulin once/twice daily + rapid-acting insulin with each meal; carbohydrate counting with insulin:carb ratio
insulin_therapy.insulin_pump_CSII: Continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion via pump; delivers basal and bolus insulin; improved flexibility and glycemic control
insulin_therapy.automated_insulin_delivery: Closed-loop 'artificial pancreas' systems (e.g., Control-IQ, Omnipod 5); CGM-integrated insulin pump with automated adjustments
insulin_therapy.targets: HbA1c <7% (<53 mmol/mol) for most adults; individualized targets; Time in Range (TIR) >70% (70-180 mg/dL)

lifestyle_management: Carbohydrate counting and insulin:carb ratio calculation
Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) use — Dexcom, Libre, Enlite
Regular physical activity with glucose monitoring adjustments
Hypoglycemia recognition and treatment (15g fast-acting carbs; glucagon kit)

short_term: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) — pH <7.3, ketones positive, anion gap metabolic acidosis; life-threatening emergency
Hypoglycemia — blood glucose <70 mg/dL; severe hypoglycemia may cause loss of consciousness, seizures
Hypoglycemia unawareness — loss of autonomic warning symptoms
long_term: Diabetic nephropathy — leading cause of chronic kidney disease; progression to ESRD
Diabetic retinopathy — leading cause of blindness in working-age adults; proliferative retinopathy, macular edema
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy — painful/painless peripheral neuropathy; diabetic foot ulcers; amputation risk
Diabetic autonomic neuropathy — gastroparesis, orthostatic hypotension, cardiac autonomic neuropathy
Cardiovascular disease — 2-5x increased risk of MI, stroke, heart failure
Celiac disease — increased co-occurrence (5% of T1DM)
Addison's disease — rare but increased autoimmune co-occurrence

prevention_methods: Primary prevention: no established prevention for T1DM autoimmunity onset in general population
Teplizumab therapy can delay clinical T1DM onset by ~2-3 years in Stage 2 T1DM (seropositive + dysglycemia)
TrialNet screening for autoantibodies in first-degree relatives
Prevention of complications through excellent glycemic control from diagnosis
lifestyle_modifications: Consistent meal timing and carbohydrate counting
Regular exercise (aerobic and resistance training with glucose monitoring)
Blood pressure control (<130/80 mmHg)
Smoking cessation
Regular foot examination and foot care
Annual ophthalmologic examination
screening_recommendations: Annual urine albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) from 5 years after diagnosis
Annual serum creatinine and eGFR
Annual dilated ophthalmologic examination (from 5 years after diagnosis or age 10, whichever earlier)
Annual foot examination and sensory testing (10-g monofilament, vibration)
Lipid panel annually
Blood pressure at every visit
Annual thyroid function testing
Celiac disease antibody screening at diagnosis and when symptomatic

prognosis.survival_rate: Modern T1DM management has dramatically improved prognosis. Life expectancy gap between T1DM and general population has narrowed significantly but remains ~10-15 years shorter in some studies. Excellent glycemic control (HbA1c near normal) prevents or delays complications (DCCT/EDIC trial).
prognosis.disease_progression: T1DM is lifelong. Without treatment, DKA occurs within days to weeks. Microvascular complications begin accumulating after 5-10 years of poor glycemic control. Intensive glycemic management can halt or reverse early nephropathy and retinopathy.
prognosis.recurrence: Not applicable as a primary disease occurrence, but islet transplantation recipients can experience graft failure and return to insulin dependence.

basic_information.definition: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by progressive insulin resistance in peripheral tissues coupled with relative insulin deficiency due to beta cell dysfunction, leading to chronic hyperglycemia.
basic_information.overview: T2DM is the most common form of diabetes, accounting for ~90-95% of all cases worldwide. It is strongly associated with obesity, physical inactivity, and aging. Unlike T1DM, T2DM involves insulin resistance rather than absolute insulin deficiency, and initially can be managed with lifestyle intervention and oral medications before insulin therapy becomes necessary. It is a major driver of cardiovascular disease, kidney failure, blindness, and limb amputation globally.

causes: Insulin resistance: impaired insulin signaling in skeletal muscle, liver, and adipose tissue
Progressive beta cell dysfunction and eventual beta cell failure
Hepatic glucose overproduction (gluconeogenesis) due to liver insulin resistance
Abnormal incretin effect (reduced GLP-1 secretion/response)
Increased glucagon secretion (alpha cell dysfunction)
Renal glucose reabsorption (increased renal glucose threshold)
Central nervous system dysregulation of appetite and glucose homeostasis
risk_factors: Overweight or obesity (BMI >25 kg/m², or lower thresholds in Asian populations)
History of gestational diabetes or delivery of baby >9 lbs
Prediabetes (impaired fasting glucose or impaired glucose tolerance)
Certain racial/ethnic groups (African American, Latino, Native American, Asian American, Pacific Islander)
Certain medications (corticosteroids, atypical antipsychotics, thiazide diuretics)

pathophysiology.disease_mechanism: T2DM results from a complex interplay of insulin resistance and beta cell dysfunction ('ominous octet' by Ralph DeFronzo). Insulin resistance in skeletal muscle reduces glucose uptake after meals. The liver fails to suppress glucose production (gluconeogenesis) despite high insulin levels. Initially, the pancreas compensates by hypersecretion of insulin, but over time beta cells fail due to glucotoxicity, lipotoxicity, endoplasmic reticulum stress, and amyloid deposition. The resulting relative insulin deficiency combined with insulin resistance causes chronic hyperglycemia.
cellular_changes: Defective IRS-1/IRS-2 insulin receptor signaling (serine phosphorylation inhibits signaling)
Impaired PI3K-AKT pathway activity in muscle and liver
Increased hepatic glucose production (PEPCK, G6Pase upregulation)
Ectopic lipid deposition in muscle and liver causing lipotoxicity
Beta cell amyloid deposition (IAPP/amylin aggregation)
Beta cell apoptosis from glucotoxicity, lipotoxicity, ER stress
Reduced beta cell mass (~50% at clinical diagnosis)
Impaired GLP-1 and GIP incretin responses
Increased alpha cell glucagon secretion — worsens postprandial hyperglycemia
Adipose tissue inflammation (M1 macrophage infiltration, pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion: TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, resistin)

types.Prediabetes: Impaired Fasting Glucose (IFG): FPG 100-125 mg/dL; Impaired Glucose Tolerance (IGT): 2h OGTT 140-199 mg/dL; HbA1c 5.7-6.4%
types.T2DM: Full type 2 diabetes with hyperglycemia meeting diagnostic thresholds
types.MODY: Maturity-Onset Diabetes of the Young — monogenic diabetes, often misclassified as T2DM; caused by single gene mutations (GCK, HNF1A, HNF4A, etc.)
types.Secondary_Diabetes: Drug-induced (corticosteroids), pancreatic disease (pancreatitis, pancreatectomy), endocrine disorders (Cushing's, acromegaly, pheochromocytoma)

early_symptoms: Often asymptomatic for years — detected on routine screening
common_symptoms: Polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia (classic triad; less dramatic than T1DM)
Weight gain or difficulty losing weight
Recurrent infections (skin, urinary, vaginal yeast infections)
Acanthosis nigricans (darkened skin folds in neck, axilla — sign of insulin resistance)
advanced_symptoms: Diabetic foot ulcers or non-healing wounds
Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (HHS) — extremely high glucose without ketosis; confusion, coma
Peripheral arterial disease symptoms (claudication, cold extremities)

physical_examination: Skin examination (acanthosis nigricans, skin tags, infections)

blood_tests: Fasting Plasma Glucose (FPG) ≥126 mg/dL (7.0 mmol/L) on two occasions
2-hour OGTT (75g glucose) ≥200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L)
HbA1c ≥6.5% (48 mmol/mol)
Random glucose ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms
C-peptide — normal or elevated (distinguishes from T1DM)
Lipid panel — triglycerides, HDL, LDL, total cholesterol
Urine albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) — nephropathy screening
Serum creatinine and eGFR — renal function
Liver function tests (fatty liver is common comorbidity)
TSH — thyroid disease screening
imaging: Carotid intima media thickness (IMT) — cardiovascular risk assessment
Coronary calcium score — cardiovascular risk stratification
Renal ultrasound — if significant nephropathy
Lower extremity Doppler — peripheral arterial disease
laboratory_tests.biopsy: Not routinely performed for T2DM diagnosis. Liver biopsy may be considered for NASH/NAFLD assessment.
laboratory_tests.histopathological_examination: Research: islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) deposition; reduced beta cell mass; beta cell hypertrophy (compensatory phase); islet fibrosis

microscopic_findings: Islet amyloid deposition (IAPP/amylin) — characteristic finding, present in >90% of T2DM
Reduced beta cell mass (~40-60% of normal)
Islet fibrosis
Beta cell hypertrophy (compensatory phase)
Relatively preserved alpha cells
Vacuolar degeneration of beta cells
Diffuse islet atrophy in long-standing T2DM
tissue_changes: Increased adipose tissue infiltration around islets
Liver: hepatic steatosis (NAFLD/NASH) very common comorbidity
cellular_characteristics: Beta cells with pale cytoplasm and enlarged ER (ER stress)
Amyloid plaques replacing islet architecture
Mitochondrial dysfunction in beta cells
Absence of significant mononuclear infiltration (unlike T1DM)

diagnostic_biomarkers: HbA1c ≥6.5% — gold standard for diagnosis and monitoring
Fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL
2-hour 75g OGTT glucose ≥200 mg/dL
Fasting insulin and C-peptide — elevated early (insulin resistance), declining later (beta cell failure)
HOMA-IR (Homeostatic Model Assessment of Insulin Resistance) — research/clinical tool
prognostic_biomarkers: HbA1c — glycemic control and complication risk
Urine albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) — early nephropathy
eGFR — renal function and CKD staging
LDL cholesterol — cardiovascular risk
Blood pressure — major modifiable cardiovascular risk factor
Ankle-brachial index (ABI) — peripheral arterial disease
hs-CRP — cardiovascular inflammation marker
NT-proBNP/BNP — heart failure risk
genetic_markers: TCF7L2 — strongest common genetic variant for T2DM risk
PPARG (Pro12Ala) — insulin sensitivity gene
KCNJ11 (E23K) — potassium channel; beta cell insulin secretion
FTO gene — obesity-associated T2DM risk
SLC30A8 — zinc transporter; beta cell function
HNF1A, HNF4A, GCK — MODY genes (monogenic diabetes mimicking T2DM)
Polygenic Risk Scores (PRS) for T2DM — research use

treatment.first_line_treatment: Lifestyle intervention (diet modification, weight loss, physical activity) plus Metformin as initial pharmacotherapy (unless contraindicated).
medications: Metformin — first-line; reduces hepatic glucose production; weight-neutral/loss; cardioprotective
GLP-1 receptor agonists: Semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy), Liraglutide (Victoza), Dulaglutide, Tirzepatide (GIP/GLP-1 dual agonist) — weight loss, cardiovascular benefit
SGLT2 inhibitors: Empagliflozin (Jardiance), Dapagliflozin (Farxiga), Canagliflozin — cardiovascular and renal protection, weight loss
DPP-4 inhibitors: Sitagliptin (Januvia), Saxagliptin, Linagliptin — weight-neutral
Sulfonylureas: Glipizide, Glimepiride, Glyburide — insulin secretagogues; hypoglycemia risk; weight gain
Thiazolidinediones (TZDs): Pioglitazone — improves insulin sensitivity; weight gain; fluid retention
Insulin: NPH, glargine, detemir, degludec; rapid-acting analogs as needed
Tirzepatide (Mounjaro) — dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist; superior HbA1c and weight reduction
oral_medications: SGLT2 inhibitors — especially if HF, CKD, or CVD present
GLP-1 RAs — especially if obesity or CVD present
treatment.insulin_therapy: Basal insulin added when oral agents insufficient (target HbA1c not achieved). Progression to basal-bolus regimen if needed. Insulin degludec (Tresiba) or glargine U-300 (Toujeo) have lower hypoglycemia risk.
lifestyle_management: Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT): caloric restriction for weight loss; carbohydrate quality and quantity; Mediterranean or DASH diet
Physical activity: 150 min/week moderate aerobic + 2x/week resistance training
Weight loss: 5-10% body weight reduction significantly improves glycemia; >15% may achieve remission
Bariatric/metabolic surgery: for BMI ≥40 or ≥35 with comorbidities; can achieve T2DM remission
Lipid management: statin therapy for most T2DM adults

short_term: Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State (HHS) — extremely high glucose (>600 mg/dL), severe dehydration, altered consciousness
Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) — rare in T2DM but possible especially with SGLT2 inhibitors (euglycemic DKA)
Medication side effects: lactic acidosis with metformin (rare), UTI/genital infections with SGLT2i
long_term: Microvascular: Diabetic nephropathy (leading cause of ESRD), Diabetic retinopathy (leading cause of blindness), Diabetic peripheral and autonomic neuropathy
Macrovascular: Coronary artery disease/MI, Stroke, Peripheral arterial disease
Diabetic foot disease: neuropathic/ischemic ulcers, Charcot arthropathy, amputation
Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) / NASH / cirrhosis
Increased cancer risk (liver, pancreas, colorectal, breast, endometrial)

prevention_methods: Lifestyle intervention in prediabetes reduces T2DM risk by 58% (Diabetes Prevention Program)
Metformin therapy in high-risk prediabetes reduces progression
Weight loss of 5-10% body weight
Regular physical activity
Healthy dietary patterns
lifestyle_modifications: Mediterranean, DASH, or low-glycemic index diet
150+ minutes of moderate physical activity per week
Achieve and maintain healthy body weight
Reduce sedentary behavior
Moderate alcohol consumption
Smoking cessation
Adequate sleep (7-9 hours)
screening_recommendations: Screen all adults ≥35 years with overweight/obesity
Screen at any age if: BMI ≥25 + one or more risk factors (ADA 2024)
Testing: FPG, 2h OGTT, or HbA1c
If normal: rescreen every 3 years; if prediabetes: rescreen annually
Screen children/adolescents with overweight + risk factors
Screen gestational diabetes patients within 4-12 weeks postpartum and every 1-3 years thereafter

prognosis.survival_rate: T2DM significantly reduces life expectancy (average ~10 years reduction without optimal management). Optimal management of glycemia, blood pressure, lipids, and lifestyle can near-normalize cardiovascular risk. Modern therapies (GLP-1 RAs, SGLT2i) have demonstrated cardiovascular and renal mortality benefits.
prognosis.disease_progression: T2DM is progressive: beta cell function declines ~4-6% per year. Most patients require addition of medications over time. After 10 years, many patients need insulin. Complications develop over years of hyperglycemia exposure; microvascular complications can appear even at prediabetes stage.
prognosis.recurrence: T2DM remission is possible with bariatric surgery (50-80% remission at 1 year) or very low-calorie diet (DiRECT trial: 46% remission at 1 year). Remission defined as HbA1c <6.5% off glucose-lowering medications for ≥3 months.

Includes: Section 3 (Prevention), Section 8 (Obesity and Weight Management), Section 9 (Pharmacologic Approaches). Available at: https://diabetesjournals.org/care/issue/47/Supplement_1

basic_information.definition: Gestational diabetes mellitus is defined as glucose intolerance with onset or first recognition during pregnancy, typically in the second or third trimester, in women not previously known to have diabetes.
basic_information.overview: GDM is the most common metabolic complication of pregnancy, affecting 6-16% of pregnancies worldwide (WHO estimate). It arises from pregnancy-induced insulin resistance exacerbating pre-existing subclinical metabolic defects in susceptible women. GDM carries significant risks for both mother (pre-eclampsia, cesarean delivery, future T2DM) and fetus/neonate (macrosomia, birth injuries, neonatal hypoglycemia, future obesity and diabetes). Most cases resolve after delivery, but affected women have up to 50% lifetime risk of developing T2DM.

causes: Pregnancy-induced insulin resistance (physiological — from placental hormones: human placental lactogen, progesterone, cortisol, estrogen, prolactin)
Failure of maternal pancreatic beta cells to adequately compensate for increased insulin demand
Underlying pre-existing insulin resistance unmasked by pregnancy
Genetic predisposition to beta cell dysfunction
risk_factors: Overweight or obesity (BMI >25 kg/m²) before pregnancy
Certain ethnic/racial groups (South Asian, East Asian, African American, Hispanic, Native American, Pacific Islander)

pathophysiology.disease_mechanism: Normal pregnancy induces progressive insulin resistance beginning in the second trimester, peaking in the third trimester, to ensure adequate glucose supply to the growing fetus. Healthy women compensate through 200-250% increase in insulin secretion. In GDM, beta cells fail to provide adequate insulin secretion to overcome placental hormone-induced insulin resistance (human placental lactogen being the dominant driver). This results in postprandial hyperglycemia that crosses the placenta via glucose transporters, causing fetal hyperinsulinemia and macrosomia.
cellular_changes: Placental hormones activate serine kinases that phosphorylate IRS-1, impairing insulin signaling
Reduced GLUT4 translocation to skeletal muscle cell membranes (decreased glucose uptake)
Increased hepatic glucose production despite elevated insulin levels
Impaired first-phase insulin secretion (earliest detectable beta cell defect)
Fetal hyperinsulinemia stimulates anabolic growth (macrosomia, organomegaly)
Adipokine dysregulation: decreased adiponectin, increased resistin, TNF-α, leptin
Placental inflammation and oxidative stress contributing to insulin resistance

types.GDM_Diet_A1: Class A1 GDM — controlled with diet and lifestyle modification alone; fasting glucose <95 mg/dL and 1h <140 mg/dL and 2h <120 mg/dL after meal; ADA classification.
types.GDM_Insulin_A2: Class A2 GDM — requires insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents to achieve glycemic targets; greater fetal/maternal risk.
types.Overt_Diabetes: Pre-existing undiagnosed diabetes discovered in first trimester — distinguished from GDM by earlier onset and higher hyperglycemia; managed as pregestational diabetes.

early_symptoms: Usually asymptomatic — detected on routine prenatal screening (24-28 weeks)
Mild polydipsia or polyuria (may be attributed to normal pregnancy)
advanced_symptoms: Symptoms related to macrosomic fetus: large fundal height, fetal movement changes
Signs of pre-eclampsia: hypertension, proteinuria, edema
Rare: DKA (very rare in GDM, more likely in unrecognized T1DM)
Fetal complications: decreased fetal movements (fetal distress), polyhydramnios

physical_examination: Symphysis-fundal height measurement — large for gestational age (macrosomia)
Weight gain monitoring — excessive gestational weight gain

blood_tests: One-Step Strategy (IADPSG/WHO): 75g OGTT at 24-28 weeks — GDM diagnosed if: Fasting ≥92 mg/dL (5.1 mmol/L) OR 1h ≥180 mg/dL (10.0 mmol/L) OR 2h ≥153 mg/dL (8.5 mmol/L)

Two-Step Strategy (ADA/ACOG): Step 1: 50g GCT (non-fasting) — if 1h ≥130/135/140 mg/dL → proceed to Step 2; Step 2: 100g OGTT fasting — GDM if 2+ values meet thresholds (Carpenter-Coustan or NDDG criteria)
HbA1c at first prenatal visit (if not recently tested) — screen for pre-existing diabetes
Fasting plasma glucose at first visit — overt diabetes if ≥126 mg/dL
Urine dipstick for glucose and protein — monitoring during visits
Complete blood count, thyroid function
Urine albumin — renal function
imaging: Obstetric ultrasound — fetal biometry (estimated fetal weight, abdominal circumference) to detect macrosomia
Doppler velocimetry — placental function assessment if growth concerns
Non-stress test (NST) and biophysical profile (BPP) — fetal well-being in poorly controlled GDM
laboratory_tests.biopsy: Not applicable in clinical GDM management.
laboratory_tests.histopathological_examination: Placental pathology post-delivery (research): increased syncytiotrophoblast oxidative stress; inflammatory changes; impaired angiogenesis; chorangiosis (increased villous vascularity from chronic hypoxia in uncontrolled GDM)

microscopic_findings: Placenta: chorangiosis (>10 capillaries per villus in >10 villi) — from chronic hyperglycemia-induced hypoxia
Placental hydrops and edema of villi
Decidual vasculopathy in severe cases with hypertension
Increased syncytiotrophoblast apoptosis
Villous immaturity (relative to gestational age)
tissue_changes: Amniotic fluid: polyhydramnios reflected in membrane changes
Neonatal pancreas: beta cell hyperplasia and hypertrophy (fetal hyperinsulinemia response)
cellular_characteristics: Fetal beta cell hyperplasia — compensatory response to maternal hyperglycemia/fetal hyperglycemia
Maternal islet hypertrophy — compensation for insulin resistance
Placental trophoblast: altered GLUT1 and GLUT3 expression
Inflammatory macrophage infiltration in placental villi

diagnostic_biomarkers: Fasting plasma glucose — key screening and diagnostic value
75g OGTT (1h and 2h glucose values) — IADPSG diagnostic criteria
HbA1c — used first trimester for screening overt diabetes; less reliable during pregnancy due to RBC turnover changes
Fructosamine — reflects 2-3 week glycemic average; useful when HbA1c unreliable
C-peptide — reflects residual beta cell function
prognostic_biomarkers: Fasting hyperglycemia >95 mg/dL — higher risk of adverse outcomes; likely needs medication
Degree of hyperglycemia at diagnosis — predictive of insulin requirement
Gestational weight gain — excess predicts macrosomia
HbA1c postpartum — predicts future T2DM risk
Adiponectin levels — low levels predict GDM and future T2DM
Ferritin — high levels associated with GDM risk
Vitamin D levels — deficiency associated with GDM
genetic_markers: TCF7L2 — strongest T2DM risk gene; also increases GDM risk
MTNR1B — melatonin receptor; regulates insulin secretion; GDM risk gene
GCK (glucokinase) — mutations cause MODY2, presenting as GDM
IRS1 — insulin receptor substrate gene variants
PPARG, KCNJ11 — overlap with T2DM risk genes
HLA risk alleles — some GDM cases represent latent T1DM (LADA) onset in pregnancy

treatment.first_line_treatment: Blood glucose self-monitoring + Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT) + moderate physical activity. Insulin therapy added if glycemic targets not met within 1-2 weeks on lifestyle alone.
medications: Insulin — gold standard pharmacotherapy in GDM; does not cross placenta; NPH (intermediate-acting) and regular insulin; rapid analogs (Lispro, Aspart) safe in pregnancy
Metformin — used in some countries (UK, Australia) as oral alternative; crosses placenta; long-term neonatal safety data reassuring but still controversial per ADA
Glyburide (Glibenclamide) — oral sulfonylurea; does cross placenta; less preferred; associated with higher neonatal hypoglycemia risk
treatment.insulin_therapy: Starting doses based on weight and trimester. Typically: NPH at bedtime for fasting hyperglycemia; rapid-acting analog before meals for postprandial control. Dose adjustment weekly based on glucose logs. Target: Fasting <95 mg/dL (5.3 mmol/L); 1h postmeal <140 mg/dL (7.8 mmol/L); 2h postmeal <120 mg/dL (6.7 mmol/L) — per ADA.
lifestyle_management: Medical Nutrition Therapy: carbohydrate distribution throughout day; avoid refined sugars; adequate protein and healthy fat; 1800-2500 kcal/day adjusted to BMI; carbohydrate 35-45% of calories
Physical activity: 30 min moderate intensity activity (walking) daily or most days
Blood glucose self-monitoring: 4-6 times daily (fasting + after meals)
Weight gain targets: <5 kg additional weight gain if obese
Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) in insulin-requiring GDM

short_term: Maternal: Pre-eclampsia (2-4x increased risk), polyhydramnios, cesarean delivery, urinary tract infections, preterm labor

Fetal/Neonatal: Macrosomia (birthweight >4kg), birth trauma (shoulder dystocia, brachial plexus injury, fractures), neonatal hypoglycemia (most common neonatal complication), neonatal respiratory distress syndrome, neonatal polycythemia, neonatal jaundice (hyperbilirubinemia), intrauterine fetal demise (rare with uncontrolled GDM)
long_term: Maternal: 50% lifetime risk of T2DM; recurrence of GDM in subsequent pregnancies (30-70%); increased metabolic syndrome risk; cardiovascular disease risk

Offspring: 5-10x increased risk of T2DM and obesity in childhood/adulthood; increased risk of metabolic syndrome; impaired cognitive development if severe uncontrolled GDM; possible increased cardiovascular risk in adulthood (Barker hypothesis/developmental programming)

prevention_methods: Pre-conception weight optimization (achieve healthy BMI before pregnancy)
Regular physical activity before and during pregnancy
Healthy diet pre-conception and during pregnancy
Management of PCOS and prediabetes before conception
Myo-inositol supplementation (evidence-emerging; may reduce GDM risk in high-risk women)
Vitamin D supplementation (observational evidence)
lifestyle_modifications: Mediterranean-style diet with complex carbohydrates, fiber, healthy fats
Regular moderate physical activity throughout pregnancy
Appropriate gestational weight gain per IOM guidelines
Breastfeeding (reduces postpartum T2DM risk for mother and reduces childhood obesity in offspring)
screening_recommendations: Universal screening at 24-28 weeks gestation — WHO, IADPSG, most guidelines
Early screening at first prenatal visit for high-risk women (BMI >30, prior GDM, strong family history, previous macrosomic baby)
Postpartum screening: 75g OGTT at 4-12 weeks postpartum (ADA 2024)
Annual follow-up with HbA1c or FPG for all GDM-affected women
Lifestyle intervention in GDM-affected women to prevent progression to T2DM
Offspring monitoring: screen children for obesity and metabolic risk from early childhood

prognosis.survival_rate: GDM itself is not life-threatening when well-managed. With appropriate treatment, outcomes approach those of non-diabetic pregnancies. Poorly controlled GDM significantly increases risk of stillbirth and neonatal complications.
prognosis.disease_progression: GDM typically resolves after delivery as placental hormones clear. However, it is a sentinel event for future metabolic disease: ~50% of women with GDM will develop T2DM within 5-10 years. Risk is highest in the first 5 years postpartum. Breastfeeding, weight loss, and physical activity reduce conversion risk.
prognosis.recurrence: GDM recurs in 30-70% of subsequent pregnancies. Risk factors for recurrence include: insulin use in prior GDM pregnancy, higher BMI, interpregnancy weight gain, Hispanic ethnicity, and shorter interpregnancy interval.

review_articles.title: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus
review_articles.authors: Plows JF, Stanley JL, Baker PN, Reynolds CM, Vickers MH
review_articles.summary: Comprehensive review of GDM pathophysiology, diagnosis, management, and short/long-term outcomes.

review_articles.title: Hyperglycemia and Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes (HAPO Study)
review_articles.summary: Landmark study establishing continuous relationship between maternal glucose and adverse perinatal outcomes, forming the basis of IADPSG diagnostic criteria.

review_articles.title: Metformin versus Insulin for the Treatment of Gestational Diabetes (MiG Trial)
review_articles.summary: Metformin was not inferior to insulin for GDM management, with good neonatal outcomes but higher rate of supplemental insulin need.

review_articles.title: Risk of Type 2 Diabetes after Gestational Diabetes Mellitus: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
review_articles.authors: Bellamy L, Casas JP, Hingorani AD, Williams D
review_articles.summary: GDM is associated with a 7-fold increased risk of developing T2DM; highest risk in first 5 years postpartum.

review_articles.title: International Association of Diabetes and Pregnancy Study Groups Recommendations on the Diagnosis and Classification of Hyperglycemia in Pregnancy (IADPSG)
review_articles.summary: IADPSG criteria for GDM diagnosis using one-step 75g OGTT based on HAPO study data.
